metadata.summary: Decision framework for selecting delivery mode (predictive, agile, or named hybrid) based on six scored dimensions. Approach mismatch detected via metrics and re-evaluated mid-project.

content.title: Agile and Hybrid Approaches
content.summary: A decision framework for selecting and tailoring a delivery mode — predictive (waterfall), agile (Scrum/Kanban), or a named hybrid pattern — based on six scored dimensions: requirement clarity, stakeholder engagement, risk tolerance, team agility, contract flexibility, and regulatory burden. "Hybrid" is never a hand-wave; the selected pattern must be named (Water-Scrum-Fall, agile-discovery-then-predictive, predictive-governance-over-agile-execution). LLMs default to "agile" for almost any prompt — force balanced scoring.

why.p: No single approach fits every project. Pure waterfall is too rigid for uncertainty; pure agile fails on fixed-price compliance work. The mismatch creates rework, missed gates, and client disputes. The six-dimension scoring model makes the approach selection auditable — the decision is traceable to project inputs, not PM preference.

list.item: Choosing a delivery mode at project kickoff given a known project context
Re-evaluating mid-project when symptoms appear (waterfall slipping repeatedly, Scrum failing on fixed-price compliance)
Designing a tailored hybrid for fixed-price clients
Authoring the Development Approach section of a PMBoK 8 plan
Coaching a team transitioning between approaches with explicit ceremony and artefact mappings
Tiny projects under 4 weeks with a single well-scoped team — any methodology overhead beats the work itself; use a checklist
Mandated-by-contract approaches (DoD waterfall, FDA validated software) — no degrees of freedom
Pure operations / BAU work with no defined start/end — use Kanban + SLAs, not project methodology
Research-heavy pre-PMF startups — approach selection is premature

section.name: Selection Dimensions

rule.statement: Score the project on all six dimensions before recommending an approach. Never recommend based on team preference or default to agile without scoring.
rule.rationale: LLMs have a heavy agile bias from training data. Unscored recommendations are PM preference, not project fit. Scoring creates an auditable decision record.

list.item: Requirement clarity — stable and well-defined favours predictive; evolving and emergent favours agile
Stakeholder engagement — limited access favours predictive; highly engaged favours agile
Risk tolerance — low (must work first time) favours predictive; high (can experiment) favours agile
Team agility — less experienced favours predictive; self-organising favours agile
Contract flexibility — fixed price/scope favours predictive; time and materials favours agile
Regulatory burden — high (FDA, DoD, banking phase gates) favours predictive; low favours agile

section.reference: Six-dimension scoring script, outputs recommendation JSON

rule.statement: When recommending "hybrid", always name the specific pattern. Generic "hybrid" in output must be rejected and re-prompted.
rule.rationale: Generic hybrid recommendations produce mixed signals — teams implement waterfall with daily standups (overhead without benefit). A named pattern specifies which phase uses which method.

list.item: Water-Scrum-Fall: Plan phase (predictive) → Build phase (Scrum) → Deploy phase (predictive). Caps agile benefit — total cycle time is still long.
Agile-discovery-then-predictive: Agile discovery phase to clarify requirements → Waterfall implementation once scope is locked. Good for fixed-price contracts with unclear initial scope.
Predictive-governance-over-agile-execution: Phase gates and funding approvals are predictive; sprint execution within each phase is agile. Two reporting cadences — sprint review + phase gate.

example.note: Hybrid for a $100K fixed-price contract over 4 months.
example.code:
Phase 1: Discovery (2 weeks) — Agile - User story workshops, prototype key screens, finalise scope Phase 2: Development (10 weeks) — Scrum - 5 two-week sprints, weekly client demos, backlog refinement Phase 3: Deployment (2 weeks) — Predictive - Fixed test plan, staged rollout, documentation
Governance: - Monthly steering committee (predictive gate) - Weekly sprint reviews (agile cadence) - Change requests required for scope changes (predictive control)

antipattern.description: Agile-in-name-only: daily standups plus a waterfall plan.
antipattern.reason: Standups without a working sprint backlog and empirical planning produce the overhead of both methods with the benefit of neither.

antipattern.description: Recommending agile on a fixed-price contract without a scope-envelope warning.
antipattern.reason: Fixed-price plus agile only works with a scope-envelope contract (range of stories, change budget, exit clauses). Without it, every backlog refinement is a contract dispute.

antipattern.description: Adopting SAFe to fix a single-team velocity problem.
antipattern.reason: SAFe is a multi-team scaling framework. Applying it to a one-team problem adds PI planning, ARTs, and RTEs without solving the velocity root cause.

section.name: Scrum Framework
section.p: Product Backlog → Sprint Planning → Sprint (2 weeks) → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective → repeat.

list.item: Sprint Planning (2-4 h): select stories for sprint goal, break into tasks
Daily Scrum (15 min): synchronise, surface blockers — not a status report
Sprint Review (1-2 h): demo increment to stakeholders, update backlog
Sprint Retrospective (1-2 h): inspect process, commit to one improvement

rule.statement: A sprint review without a working software demo is a status meeting. A daily standup without an updated sprint board is a social call. Name the artefact, not just the ceremony.
rule.rationale: Ceremony names without evidence of their output signals agile-in-name-only — the team gets overhead without feedback loops.

list.item: Visualise the workflow — every work item on the board, every state visible
Limit WIP per column — enforce with card-colour rules or tooling, not social pressure
Manage flow — measure lead time and cycle time; optimise throughput, not utilisation
Make policies explicit — Definition of Done per column is written, not assumed
Improve collaboratively — retrospect on flow metrics, not feelings

rule.statement: Set WIP limits in the tool, not in a policy document. Limits enforced only by culture collapse under deadline pressure within two weeks.
rule.rationale: WIP limits are the primary Kanban control mechanism. Visual enforcement in the tool makes violations visible to the whole team without a PM having to call them out.

rule.statement: Track these metrics to detect approach mismatch: lead time (total end-to-end), velocity stability (coefficient of variation), change-request rate per sprint, and defect-escape rate. If two consecutive sprints trend wrong on any metric, re-run approach scoring.
rule.rationale: Approach mismatches manifest in metrics before they manifest in project reports. Early re-evaluation avoids a 3-month commitment to a failing pattern.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: The agent is a methodology selector + tailor. Phase 1: a `methodology-selector` reads a project context YAML (requirement clarity, stakeholder availability, contract type, team experience, risk tolerance, regulator presence) and returns a recommended approach with rationale. Phase 2: a `methodology-tailor` produces the concrete artefact list (charter / backlog / sprints / gates) and ceremony cadence. Human review between phases. Re-run on every steering committee that changes the inputs.

Agile-in-name-only is the most common failure: daily standups + waterfall plan = no benefit, doubled overhead. Water-Scrum-Fall caps the agile benefit: the build is iterative but plan and deploy phases are rigid, so total cycle time is still long. Fixed-price + agile only works with a "scope envelope" contract; LLMs miss this nuance and recommend agile to fixed-price clients without warning. Hybrid governance creates two reporting cadences (sprint review + phase gate) that both consume PM time; not free. Scrum at scale (SAFe, LeSS, Nexus) is its own beast and is *not* a generic hybrid; agents conflate them. Kanban WIP limits get violated under deadline pressure; methodology guidance does not enforce them — needs tooling. "Choose by team experience" advice creates a chicken-and-egg: junior teams default to predictive but never learn agile.

section.name: Prompt Pattern for Agent Selection
section.p: Score the project on each axis (1-5): requirement_clarity, stakeholder_engagement, risk_tolerance, team_agility, contract_flexibility, regulatory_burden. Recommend ONE of: [predictive, scrum, kanban, scrumban, water-scrum-fall, predictive-governance-over-agile, agile-discovery-then-predictive]. Output JSON: { "scores": {...}, "recommendation": "...", "rationale": ["...", "..."], "ceremonies": [{"name":"...", "cadence":"weekly"}], "artefacts": ["...", "..."], "risks_of_recommendation": ["..."], "fall_back": "..." }. Constraints: Cite at least one risk if recommending agile on a fixed-price contract. Never recommend "pure scrum" if regulatory_burden ≥ 4.

list.item: Pick the smallest approach that solves the symptom; do not adopt SAFe to fix a one-team velocity problem.
Make the choice explicit and dated in the project charter; write down the trigger that would force a re-evaluation.
For hybrids, pick *one* primary cadence (sprint OR gate) and make the other an overlay; running both at equal weight burns out the PM.
Translate ceremonies, not labels: a "sprint review" with no demo is just a status meeting.
For fixed-price agile, write a scope-envelope contract: range of stories, change-budget, exit clauses.
Track approach-fit metrics: lead time, velocity stability, change-request rate per sprint, defect-escape; if they trend wrong, revisit.
Solopreneur: default to personal Kanban with WIP=3; only graduate to Scrum once you have ≥ 2 collaborators.
LLMs default to "agile" for almost any prompt; bias toward agile is heavy in training data. Force balanced scoring.
Agents conflate Scrum, SAFe, and "agile" — pin a fixed vocabulary.
"Hybrid" is used as a hand-wave; agents should always name a specific pattern (Water-Scrum-Fall, agile-discovery-then-predictive, etc.).
Agents recommend daily standups for solo work — guard with team-size threshold.
Compliance / regulator constraints are dropped silently; require explicit input field and reject "pure agile" when burden ≥ 4.
"Fixed price + agile" gets recommended without a scope-envelope warning; force a risks-of-recommendation field.
For approach changes, agents skip the transition cost (training, tool migration); always include a transition-cost section.
Sprint length defaults to 2 weeks regardless of context; for distributed / regulated teams, 3-4 weeks is often better.

template.purpose: Sprint planning document with capacity table, backlog, dependencies, risks
Kanban board state template with WIP limits
Python: score six dimensions, recommend predictive/agile/hybrid
